context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on the draft climate action plan prospectus for my case study city, and I’m kind of stuck on how to turn my SWOT/barriers notes into 2 actual policy components. Could you help me figure out a good structure and maybe how to make the EJ part specific instead of generic?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before we dig in, can you tell me which city you're working with and share the key findings from your SWOT analysis and barriers work? That'll help us figure out where to go from here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a broad opening question about structure and EJ specificity. Rather than explaining the structure (which is already provided in the assignment), I should redirect them to what they already have and ask them to share their work so far. This follows guided discovery and Socratic method - I need to know their starting point before I can ask productive questions. I'll keep it brief and ask them to share their SWOT/barriers notes and their chosen city.

exchanges.student: I’m using Jakarta. From my SWOT/barriers notes, the big issues were flooding, land subsidence, heat, and a lot of inequality in who gets protection and services. I think the barriers were fragmented governance, limited funding, informal settlements being left out, and maybe weak enforcement, but I’m not totally sure how to turn that into 2 focused components.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid set of issues to work with. Looking at your list — flooding, subsidence, heat, inequality in protection, informal settlements being excluded — which two of these feel most connected to each other, and why? That relationship might be your starting point for choosing your two components.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good raw material to work with. Rather than guiding them toward specific components, I should help them see how to prioritize and focus from their own notes using a Socratic question. The assignment asks them to focus on EJ implications, vulnerabilities, and overlaps between components. I'll ask them to identify which issues feel most interconnected, which naturally leads them toward discovering good component pairings on their own. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think flooding and land subsidence are the most connected, since subsidence makes flooding worse. Heat also matters, but it feels a little separate unless I do something around housing or public health. I’m just not sure if my two components are supposed to be totally different topics or if they can overlap a lot.
exchanges.tutor: Actually, the assignment itself answers this question — take another look at what it says about the 'Interactions' section. What does that suggest to you about whether overlap between components is a problem or a feature?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a good question about overlap, and importantly, the assignment explicitly says to look for 'overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components' and 'potential synergies.' I should point them back to the assignment language rather than answering directly, and then push them to think about what the Interactions section is for. This is a discovery moment.

exchanges.student: Oh, right — it says to include interactions and overlaps between climate action plan components, so overlap is probably a feature, not a problem. So I guess my two components can be connected as long as they’re still distinct policies. Does that mean I should maybe do one infrastructure-type policy and one community/governance policy?
exchanges.tutor: That's an interesting framing. How well does it fit your Jakarta findings specifically? Look back at your barriers — fragmented governance, informal settlements being left out — where do those land in your infrastructure vs. community/governance split?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a good inference about overlap being a feature. Now they're proposing a framework (infrastructure vs. community/governance) which is reasonable, but I shouldn't validate it too quickly or too directly - I want them to test it against their own material. I'll ask them to check that framing against their Jakarta-specific notes rather than confirming it as the right approach.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think it fits pretty well. Infrastructure could address flooding/subsidence directly, and community/governance could deal with exclusion and fragmented decision-making. I’m a little worried that “community/governance” sounds too vague though, like not a real policy yet.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is right — a policy needs to be actionable. So ask yourself: in Jakarta specifically, what would this policy actually *do*, and for whom? Try to answer that in one concrete sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has self-identified a real weakness - 'community/governance' is too vague. This is good critical self-awareness. I should validate that instinct and push them to sharpen it using their own material rather than suggesting directions. A Socratic question about what specifically the policy would do - who, what, where - will help them concretize it.

exchanges.student: Maybe something like: create a neighborhood-based flood adaptation program for informal settlements that funds drainage improvements, early warning systems, and relocation support where risk is extreme. I think that’s more concrete, but I’m not sure if it’s too many things jammed together.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real improvement — much more actionable. Now, do those three elements (drainage, early warning, relocation) feel like one coherent package to you, or like three separate policies? What's the logic that holds them together, if any?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely concrete policy idea that is much stronger than before. I should acknowledge that progress clearly. Their concern about 'too many things jammed together' is worth addressing - but rather than telling them if it is or isn't, I should ask them to evaluate whether those three elements (drainage, early warning, relocation) are distinct enough to be separate policies or whether they form a coherent package. This keeps them in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: I think the logic is “risk-based protection for the most vulnerable neighborhoods,” but yeah, it also kind of feels like I stuffed everything in there because I didn’t know what to cut. Maybe drainage + early warning fit together better, and relocation should only come in as a last-resort part, not the main thing.
exchanges.tutor: Good self-editing. You've now got a clearer second component. What about your first one — flooding/subsidence infrastructure? Can you describe it in one concrete sentence the same way you just did for the second?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is refining well and self-editing thoughtfully. This is good progress. I should affirm this and push them to now do the same clarifying work for their first component (infrastructure/flooding-subsidence), since they've been spending more time on the second. We're also at a point where they have enough to start drafting, so I should nudge them toward actually writing rather than continuing to plan abstractly.

exchanges.student: Maybe: strengthen integrated flood and land-subsidence management by expanding drainage and pumping capacity while tightening groundwater extraction controls. I think that’s concrete, but I’m not sure if “integrated management” still sounds kind of buzzwordy.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct about buzzwords is worth keeping in mind as you write, but the best way to test it is to actually draft the Context and Opportunity sections — that's where vague language gets exposed. You have two solid component ideas now. Time to start writing. Which component do you want to tackle first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced two reasonably concrete component ideas. Their self-criticism about 'integrated management' being buzzwordy is valid but this is a drafting concern - they should test it in actual writing rather than continuing to refine the one-liner in the abstract. We've had good conceptual development and it's time to push them to start writing. I'll validate their concern briefly and direct them to begin drafting, since continued abstract refinement won't help as much as seeing it in prose.

exchanges.student: Let’s do the groundwater extraction + flooding one first. I think I need help making the Context and Opportunity specific to Jakarta instead of just saying “Jakarta has flooding and subsidence,” which is too obvious.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. So what specific evidence from your research makes Jakarta's situation distinctive? Think numbers, neighborhoods, timelines, causes — what do you actually know that someone couldn't say about just any coastal city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is right that 'Jakarta has flooding and subsidence' is too generic, and they're self-aware about it. Rather than giving them specific Jakarta facts or telling them what to include, I should ask them what they already know or found in their research that could make it specific. This keeps them doing the intellectual work. If they don't have specifics, that's a signal they need to do more research, which is also useful to surface.

exchanges.student: I know North Jakarta is especially affected, and that land subsidence there is tied a lot to groundwater extraction because piped water access is uneven. I also remember that flooding risk is worsened by sea-level rise and heavy rainfall, so it’s like multiple causes stacking up. I’m fuzzy on exact numbers though, so I need help figuring out what kinds of specifics are enough if I don’t have every stat memorized.
exchanges.tutor: What you have — North Jakarta, groundwater extraction tied to unequal piped water access, stacking causes — is already more specific than most generic descriptions. If you need exact figures, that's a short research task. But the best move now is to draft the Context section with what you have and flag where you'd add a stat. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good qualitative specifics but is worried about lacking exact statistics. This is a reasonable concern but also potentially a stalling tactic. I should validate what they have, note that specific details strengthen credibility, and push them toward actually drafting rather than continuing to plan. I should also point out that if they need specific data, that's a research task they need to do - not something I'll provide. Keep it brief and action-oriented.
